Question 4727 [English] - ID: sat-doc-032
Difficulty: hard | Domain: Reading Comprehension | Skill: Inferences
------------------------------------------------------------
The following text is adapted from Maria Cristina Mena's 1914 short story "The Vine-Leaf." It is a saying in the capital of Mexico that Dr. Malsufrido carries more family secrets under his hat than any archbishop. The doctor's hat is, appropriately enough, uncommonly capacious, rising very high, and sinking so low that it seems to be supported by his ears and eyebrows, and it has a furry look, as if it had been brushed the wrong way, which is perhaps what happens to it if it is ever brushed at all. When the doctor takes it off, the family secrets do not fly out like a flock of parrots, but remain nicely bottled up beneath a dome of old and highly polished ivory. Based on the text, how do people in the capital of Mexico most likely regard Dr. Malsufrido?

  A. Many have come to tolerate him despite his disheveled appearance.
  B. Few feel concerned that he will divulge their confidences. ✓ CORRECT
  C. Some dislike how freely he discusses his own family.
  D. Most would be unimpressed by him were it not for his professional expertise.

Explanation: The passage says secrets "remain nicely bottled up" when he removes his hat--they don't "fly out like parrots." This metaphor suggests he keeps confidences well. (B) captures this: people trust he won't reveal their secrets.

Question 4728 [English] - ID: sat-doc-033
Difficulty: hard | Domain: Reading Comprehension | Skill: Command of Evidence
------------------------------------------------------------
Mosasaurs were large marine reptiles that lived in the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 100 million to 66 million years ago. Celina Suarez, Alberto Perez-Huerta, and T. Lynn Harrell Jr. examined oxygen-18 isotopes in mosasaur tooth enamel in order to calculate likely mosasaur body temperatures and determined that mosasaurs were endothermic--that is, they used internal metabolic processes to maintain a stable body temperature in a variety of ambient temperatures. Suarez, Perez-Huerta, and Harrell claim that endothermy would have enabled mosasaurs to include relatively cold polar waters in their range. Which finding, if true, would most directly support Suarez, Perez-Huerta, and Harrell's claim?

  A. Mosasaurs' likely body temperatures are easier to determine from tooth enamel oxygen-18 isotope data than the body temperatures of nonendothermic Late Cretaceous marine reptiles are.
  B. Fossils of both mosasaurs and nonendothermic marine reptiles have been found in roughly equal numbers in regions known to be near the poles during the Late Cretaceous, though in lower concentrations than elsewhere.
  C. Several mosasaur fossils have been found in regions known to be near the poles during the Late Cretaceous, while relatively few fossils of nonendothermic marine reptiles have been found in those locations. ✓ CORRECT
  D. During the Late Cretaceous, seawater temperatures were likely higher throughout mosasaurs' range, including near the poles, than seawater temperatures at those same latitudes are today.

Explanation: The claim is that endothermy allowed mosasaurs to live in cold polar waters. If (C) is true--mosasaurs found near poles while non-endothermic reptiles were not--this supports the advantage of endothermy for surviving cold environments.


================================================================================

Question 4729 [English] - ID: sat-doc-034
Difficulty: hard | Domain: Reading Comprehension | Skill: Command of Evidence
------------------------------------------------------------
Almost all works of fiction contain references to the progression of time, including the time of day when events in a story take place. In a 2020 study, Allen Kim, Charuta Pethe, and Steven Skiena claim that an observable pattern in such references reflects a shift in human behavior prompted by the spread of electric lighting in the late nineteenth century. The researchers drew this conclusion from an analysis of more than 50,000 novels spanning many centuries and cultures, using software to recognize and tally both specific time references--that is, clock phrases, such as 7 a.m. or 2:30 p.m.--and implied ones, such as mentions of meals typically associated with a particular time of day. Which finding from the study, if true, would most directly support the researchers' conclusion?

  A. Novels published after the year 1800 include the clock phrase 10 a.m. less often than novels published before the year 1800 do.
  B. Novels published after 1880 contain significantly more references to activities occurring after 10 p.m. than do novels from earlier periods. ✓ CORRECT
  C. Among novels published in the nineteenth century, implied time references become steadily more common than clock phrases as publication dates approach 1900.
  D. The time references of noon (12 p.m.) and midnight (12 a.m.) are used with roughly the same frequency in the novels.

Explanation: The claim is that electric lighting changed behavior patterns in fiction. Electric lighting enables late-night activity. If novels after 1880 (when electric lights spread) show more references to activities after 10 p.m., this supports the claim. (B) provides this evidence.


================================================================================

Question 4730 [English] - ID: sat-doc-035
Difficulty: hard | Domain: Reading Comprehension | Skill: Inferences
------------------------------------------------------------
One challenge when researching whether holding elected office changes a person's behavior is the problem of ensuring that the experiment has an appropriate control group. To reveal the effect of holding office, researchers must compare people who hold elected office with people who do not hold office but who are otherwise similar to the office-holders. Since researchers are unable to control which politicians win elections, they therefore ______. Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. struggle to find valid data about the behavior of politicians who do not currently hold office.
  B. can only conduct valid studies with people who have previously held office rather than people who presently hold office.
  C. should select a control group of people who differ from office-holders in several significant ways.
  D. will find it difficult to identify a group of people who can function as an appropriate control group for their studies. ✓ CORRECT

Explanation: The passage establishes that researchers need a control group of people "otherwise similar" to office-holders. But they can't control who wins elections. This makes it difficult to find similar non-office-holders. (D) captures this challenge.

  C. Recycling is important. ✓ CORRECT
  D. I believe in my opinion that recycling is important.

Explanation: "In my opinion" and "I think" are redundant. The statement is strongest without these hedges.

  C. The storm was powerful it knocked down many trees.

  D. The job requires write, edit, and proofread documents.

Which revision best clarifies the logical relationship? "The medication was highly effective; nevertheless, it caused severe side effects."

  C. Although the medication was highly effective, severe side effects limited its use. ✓ CORRECT
  D. The medication was highly effective, and it caused severe side effects.

Explanation: "Although" establishes clear concession, and "limited its use" shows the consequence of the contrast.

  D. Financial decisions should be made quickly

Explanation: This idiom means continuing to invest in something that has already proven unsuccessful.

Question 4900 [English] - ID: sat-eng-120

------------------------------------------------------------
The civil rights movement of the 1960s achieved landmark legislative victories through a convergence of factors. While individual activists demonstrated remarkable courage and determination, historians emphasize that lasting change required more than personal sacrifice. The economic prosperity of the postwar era created conditions where reforms became politically feasible, but the decisive breakthrough came when grassroots organizing intersected with federal policy initiatives. The Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965 emerged from sustained pressure by organizations like the NAACP and SNCC working in concert with sympathetic legislators and a shifting national consciousness.

  A. Individual effort and determination
  B. Favorable economic conditions
  C. A combination of policy changes and social movements ✓ CORRECT
  D. Pure chance and coincidence

Explanation: The passage emphasizes that "the decisive breakthrough came when grassroots organizing intersected with federal policy initiatives," highlighting the combination of social movements and policy changes.

Question 4915 [English] - ID: sat-eng-135
Difficulty: Medium | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Identifying Limitations
------------------------------------------------------------
A team of researchers at Stanford University investigated the relationship between sleep quality and academic performance among college students. The study followed 47 undergraduates over one semester, tracking their sleep patterns through wearable devices and correlating these data with their grade point averages. The results showed a statistically significant positive correlation between consistent sleep schedules and higher grades. However, the researchers acknowledged that their sample, drawn exclusively from students in introductory psychology courses at a single institution, limits how widely these findings can be applied to the broader college population.

According to the passage, the primary limitation of the study was:

  A. Researchers were biased toward certain outcomes.
  B. The sample size was too small to draw generalizable conclusions. ✓ CORRECT
  C. The study took too long to complete.
  D. The research questions were poorly formulated.

Explanation: The passage explicitly states that the sample "limits how widely these findings can be applied," indicating the small, non-representative sample was the primary limitation.

Question 4916 [English] - ID: sat-eng-136

------------------------------------------------------------
Which revision eliminates the double negative? "I don't have no money."

  A. I don't have any money. ✓ CORRECT
  B. I don't got no money.
  C. I haven't got no money.
  D. I ain't got no money.

Explanation: "Any" replaces "no" to eliminate the double negative while maintaining the intended meaning.


================================================================================

------------------------------------------------------------
The author's use of understatement in describing the disaster as "somewhat inconvenient" serves to:

  A. Minimize the importance of the event
  B. Create ironic emphasis on the event's severity ✓ CORRECT
  C. Show that the author was unaffected
  D. Demonstrate poor word choice

Explanation: Understatement creates irony by describing something serious in deliberately mild terms.

Question 4922 [English] - ID: sat-eng-142
Difficulty: Medium | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Central Ideas
------------------------------------------------------------
The history of technological advancement reveals a consistent pattern: each breakthrough that solves existing problems simultaneously introduces new complications. The automobile liberated people from the constraints of horse-drawn transport but created traffic congestion and air pollution. The internet democratized access to information while enabling unprecedented threats to privacy and the spread of misinformation. Even life-saving medical innovations, such as antibiotics, have generated new challenges in the form of resistant bacteria. This cycle of solution and complication does not argue against innovation—rather, it suggests that progress requires ongoing adaptation and vigilance.

The passage suggests that technological innovation often:

  A. Solves all problems without creating new ones
  B. Creates new challenges while addressing existing ones ✓ CORRECT
  C. Has no significant impact on society
  D. Should be avoided whenever possible

Explanation: The passage emphasizes that "each breakthrough that solves existing problems simultaneously introduces new complications," directly supporting option B.


================================================================================

Question 4923 [English] - ID: sat-eng-143
Difficulty: Medium | Domain: Expression of Ideas | Skill: Logical Conclusions
------------------------------------------------------------
Climate change presents a challenge that no single entity can solve alone. Individual lifestyle changes, while valuable, cannot offset industrial emissions at scale. Government regulations face resistance from economic interests invested in the status quo. Corporate sustainability initiatives, though increasingly common, often prioritize public relations over substantive action. Scientific organizations continue to refine our understanding of climate dynamics, yet translating research into policy remains difficult. ______

Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. In conclusion, more research is clearly needed in unrelated fields.
  B. Therefore, addressing this issue requires coordinated action from multiple stakeholders. ✓ CORRECT
  C. However, none of this really matters.
  D. Meanwhile, other topics deserve more attention.

Explanation: The paragraph describes how no single group can solve the problem alone, so the logical conclusion is that coordinated action from multiple stakeholders is required.


================================================================================

Question 4924 [English] - ID: sat-eng-144
Difficulty: Medium | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Idioms
------------------------------------------------------------
In discussions of educational reform, policymakers frequently debate curriculum changes, teacher training programs, and technology integration. Yet these conversations consistently avoid the elephant in the room: the profound inequalities in school funding that leave students in low-income districts with fewer resources, larger class sizes, and deteriorating facilities. Until this fundamental disparity is addressed, other reforms risk becoming mere bandages on a deeper wound.

The author refers to the "elephant in the room" to:

  A. Discuss zoo conservation efforts
  B. Address an obvious problem that others have avoided discussing ✓ CORRECT
  C. Describe the room's physical layout
  D. Criticize excessive focus on wildlife

Explanation: The author uses the idiom to highlight that funding inequality is an obvious, significant problem that educational reform discussions typically avoid.

Question 4988 [English] - ID: add-eng-058

------------------------------------------------------------
The building was constructed in 1920; _____, it has undergone several renovations.

  A. consequently
  B. since then ✓ CORRECT
  C. therefore
  D. instead

Explanation: 'Since then' indicates events that occurred after 1920.

The volcano erupted suddenly. It caused widespread destruction throughout the region.

  A. The volcano erupted suddenly, and it caused widespread destruction throughout the region.
  B. Erupting suddenly, the volcano caused widespread destruction throughout the region.
  C. The volcano erupted suddenly, causing widespread destruction throughout the region. ✓ CORRECT
  D. The volcano, erupting suddenly, caused widespread destruction throughout the region.

Explanation: Option C uses a participial phrase to combine the sentences smoothly and concisely.

Question 5044 [English] - ID: add-eng-114
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Expression of Ideas | Skill: Evidence and Analysis
------------------------------------------------------------
Which choice best uses evidence to support a complex claim?

Claim: Economic inequality has both causes and consequences that reinforce each other.

  A. Economic inequality is bad.
  B. Research shows that unequal access to education perpetuates income disparities across generations, while concentrated wealth enables the affluent to influence policies that further entrench their advantages, creating a self-reinforcing cycle. ✓ CORRECT
  C. Some people are rich and some are poor.
  D. Inequality exists in many countries.

Explanation: Provides specific mechanisms showing the reinforcing cycle described in the claim.

Question 5045 [English] - ID: add-eng-115
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Expression of Ideas | Skill: Argumentation
------------------------------------------------------------
Which revision most effectively addresses a gap in the argument?

Original argument: "Students should study abroad because it's a valuable experience."

Gap: No specific benefits are mentioned.

  A. Students should study abroad because it's really valuable.
  B. Studying abroad offers transformative benefits: developing cross-cultural competencies increasingly demanded by employers, gaining language proficiency, and cultivating adaptability—skills that a 2023 survey found boosted graduates' starting salaries by 15%. ✓ CORRECT
  C. Many students choose to study abroad.
  D. Studying abroad is popular and valuable.

Explanation: Provides specific, quantified benefits to fill the gap in the original argument.

Which choice most effectively connects ideas across paragraphs?

Current paragraph opener needs to transition to discussing social reforms.

  A. Social reforms were important.
  B. The crowded, unsanitary conditions created by rapid industrialization inevitably sparked demands for change, giving rise to sweeping social reforms that would reshape urban life. ✓ CORRECT
  C. Meanwhile, other things were happening.
  D. Social reforms began at some point.

Explanation: Links industrial conditions (previous topic) to social reforms (new topic).

Question 5047 [English] - ID: add-eng-117

Which revision most effectively strengthens the logical connection between cause and effect?

  B. Following implementation of community policing initiatives, which increased trust between officers and residents, reported crimes declined 23% as neighborhoods became more willing to cooperate with law enforcement. ✓ CORRECT
  C. The policy worked and crime went down.
  D. Crime decreased after the policy started.

Explanation: Explains the mechanism (trust → cooperation) connecting policy to outcome.

Explanation: 'Which' introduces non-restrictive clauses set off by commas.

Proponents argue that reimagining urban spaces can reduce carbon emissions, improve public health, and create more vibrant communities.

  A. contrast two competing theories about urban development
  B. trace the historical evolution of city planning

  D. analyze the economic factors influencing housing policy

  A. a litotes understating the significance of omission
  B. an oxymoron highlighting paradoxical visibility ✓ CORRECT
  C. an apophasis drawing attention through denial
  D. a paraprosdokian subverting expectations

Explanation: The phrase is oxymoronic—something absent cannot literally be conspicuous, yet its very absence makes it noticeable, creating a paradox of visibility.

  C. reinforce the inevitability of the conclusion ✓ CORRECT
  D. demonstrate mastery of classical rhetoric

Explanation: Epistrophe (ending repetition) creates rhythm, builds thematic unity, and most importantly emphasizes inevitability—the conclusion feels inescapable.


================================================================================

Question 5234 [English] - ID: exp004
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Expression of Ideas | Skill: Sentence Structure
------------------------------------------------------------
Which revision most effectively employs periodic sentence structure to build suspense?

Original: 'The detective finally revealed the murderer after questioning all suspects, reviewing evidence, and considering every possibility.'

  A. The detective, after questioning all suspects, finally revealed the murderer.
  B. Finally, after all the questioning and reviewing, the murderer was revealed by the detective.
  C. Questioning suspects, reviewing evidence, considering possibilities—the detective revealed the murderer.
  D. After questioning all suspects, reviewing evidence, and considering every possibility, the detective finally revealed the murderer. ✓ CORRECT

Explanation: Periodic sentences delay the main clause until the end, building suspense. D places all subordinate elements first, withholding the revelation until the final position.


================================================================================

Question 5235 [English] - ID: exp005
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Syntactic Analysis
------------------------------------------------------------
The passage's shift from paratactic to hypotactic syntax in the final section suggests:

  A. a transition from observation to analysis ✓ CORRECT
  B. increasing complexity of thought
  C. the narrator's growing uncertainty
  D. a movement from action to reflection

Explanation: Parataxis (simple, coordinated clauses) suits observation; hypotaxis (complex, subordinated clauses) suits analysis. The shift marks movement from describing to interpreting.


================================================================================

Question 5236 [English] - ID: exp006
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Rhetorical Devices
------------------------------------------------------------
The author's use of anadiplosis ('Fear leads to anger. Anger leads to hate.') creates:

  A. emotional escalation via structural mirroring
  B. logical progression through linked repetition ✓ CORRECT
  C. causal chain emphasized by terminal-initial echo
  D. rhythmic momentum building toward climax

Explanation: Anadiplosis (repeating the end of one clause at the start of the next) creates logical progression through linked repetition, making the causal sequence feel inevitable.


================================================================================

Question 5237 [English] - ID: exp007
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Standard English Conventions | Skill: Punctuation
------------------------------------------------------------
Which choice best describes the function of the em-dash in: 'The solution—if one could call it that—satisfied no one'?

  A. emphatic interruption signaling doubt
  B. dramatic pause heightening skepticism
  C. syntactic isolation of conditional hedging ✓ CORRECT
  D. parenthetical qualification undermining the noun

Explanation: The em-dashes syntactically isolate the conditional phrase 'if one could call it that,' which hedges the appropriateness of 'solution'—a precise grammatical function.

Question 5238 [English] - ID: exp008

The passage's anaphoric structure ('We shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds, we shall fight in the fields') primarily serves to:

  A. enumerate locations systematically
  B. emphasize geographical comprehensiveness
  C. establish parallel grammatical rhythm
  D. build cumulative rhetorical force ✓ CORRECT

Explanation: While anaphora creates parallelism and lists locations, its primary rhetorical purpose is building cumulative force—each repetition intensifies the message's impact.

Question 5239 [English] - ID: exp009
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Rhetorical Devices
------------------------------------------------------------
The author's chiasmic structure in 'Ask not what your country can do for you—ask what you can do for your country' achieves its effect through:

  A. syntactic inversion creating memorable contrast ✓ CORRECT
  B. reversal of grammatical elements (AB:BA pattern)
  C. antithetical meaning reinforced by mirrored form
  D. parallel structure with inverted word order

Explanation: Chiasmus works through syntactic inversion that creates a memorable contrast. The reversal pattern makes the message stick in the reader's mind.

Question 5240 [English] - ID: exp010
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Rhetorical Devices
------------------------------------------------------------
The strategic use of asyndeton in 'I came, I saw, I conquered' creates:

  A. emphatic separation of distinct achievements
  B. breathless pace suggesting swift action ✓ CORRECT
  C. rhetorical compression mirroring military efficiency
  D. staccato rhythm emphasizing each verb

Explanation: Asyndeton (omitting conjunctions) creates a breathless pace suggesting swift action—the lack of 'and' between clauses speeds the rhythm dramatically.

Question 5241 [English] - ID: exp011
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Standard English Conventions | Skill: Verb Mood
------------------------------------------------------------
Which sentence demonstrates correct use of the subjunctive mood in a contrary-to-fact condition?

  A. If I was wealthy, I would donate more to charity.
  B. If I am wealthy, I will donate more to charity.
  C. If I were wealthy, I would donate more to charity. ✓ CORRECT
  D. If I be wealthy, I would donate more to charity.

Explanation: Contrary-to-fact conditions require the past subjunctive 'were' for all persons. 'If I were' correctly expresses a hypothetical contrary to present reality.

The passage's polysyndeton ('and the rain fell and the wind blew and the thunder roared') contrasts with typical journalistic prose to:

  A. slow the pace and emphasize each element
  B. suggest overwhelming accumulation of forces
  C. create biblical or epic register
  D. establish a formal, elevated tone ✓ CORRECT

Explanation: Polysyndeton (repeated conjunctions) establishes a formal, elevated tone that contrasts with journalistic prose, evoking biblical and classical traditions.

Question 5243 [English] - ID: exp013
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Figurative Language
------------------------------------------------------------
In the phrase 'a deafening silence,' the author employs:

  A. oxymoron for paradoxical emphasis ✓ CORRECT
  B. catachresis for striking effect
  C. synesthesia blending sensory modalities
  D. hypallage transferring epithets

Explanation: Oxymoron combines contradictory terms ('deafening' and 'silence') for paradoxical effect. While synesthesia involves senses, here it's the contradiction that creates meaning.

Question 5244 [English] - ID: exp014

  A. suggest reluctance overcome by other factors
  B. express cautious affirmation through double negative ✓ CORRECT
  C. maintain formal register through understatement
  D. create ambiguity about the subject's true feelings

Explanation: Litotes (understatement via negation) expresses cautious affirmation through double negative, conveying willingness while maintaining restraint.

Question 5245 [English] - ID: exp015

------------------------------------------------------------
Which revision correctly maintains the passage's established free indirect discourse?

  A. She thought that he was being unreasonable.
  B. 'He is being unreasonable,' she thought.
  C. He was being unreasonable. How could he not see? ✓ CORRECT
  D. She found his behavior unreasonable in her opinion.

Explanation: Free indirect discourse blends narrator and character perspectives without quotation marks or explicit attribution. C presents the character's thoughts through the narrator's voice.

Question 5246 [English] - ID: exp016
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Advanced Figurative Language
------------------------------------------------------------
The author's deployment of metalepsis—where the effect is named to imply the cause—in 'the gray hairs of experience' demonstrates:

  A. metonymic substitution of result for process
  B. synecdochic representation of age through physical sign
  C. metaphoric equation of appearance with wisdom
  D. complex figuration involving multiple tropes ✓ CORRECT

Explanation: The phrase operates on multiple levels: metonymy (effect for cause), synecdoche (part for whole), and metaphor (gray hair equals wisdom)—demonstrating layered figuration.

Question 5247 [English] - ID: exp017
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Syntactic Effects
------------------------------------------------------------
The passage's anacoluthon ('The government—but what does it matter now?—decided to proceed') creates:

  A. syntactic rupture mimicking thought interruption ✓ CORRECT
  B. performative demonstration of the speaker's resignation
  C. tension between logical structure and emotional reality
  D. formal disruption reflecting psychological fragmentation

Explanation: Anacoluthon (grammatical break mid-sentence) mimics interrupted thought—the syntactic rupture directly performs the mental interruption it describes.


================================================================================

Question 5248 [English] - ID: exp018
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Rhetorical Devices
------------------------------------------------------------
The author's use of praeteritio ('I will not mention his numerous failures') functions as:

  A. ironic emphasis through feigned omission
  B. plausible deniability while making accusations ✓ CORRECT
  C. rhetorical have-it-both-ways strategy
  D. subtle attack disguised as restraint

Explanation: Praeteritio (mentioning by claiming not to mention) provides plausible deniability while making accusations—the speaker can claim they didn't make the charge.


================================================================================

Question 5249 [English] - ID: exp019
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Rhetorical Devices
------------------------------------------------------------
The strategic placement of a syllepsis in 'She took his advice and his wallet' creates:

  A. comedic bathos through unexpected descent
  B. characterization through juxtaposed actions
  C. zeugmatic yoking of abstract and concrete under one verb ✓ CORRECT
  D. ironic contrast between trust and betrayal

Explanation: Syllepsis (one word in different senses with two objects) achieves zeugmatic yoking—'took' means different things with 'advice' (followed) versus 'wallet' (stole).


================================================================================

Question 5250 [English] - ID: exp020
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Syntactic Analysis
------------------------------------------------------------
The passage's sustained use of hypotaxis reaching four levels of subordination suggests:

  A. Byzantine complexity of the subject matter
  B. the narrator's tendency toward qualification
  C. mimetic representation of entangled circumstances
  D. the interpretation depends on surrounding context ✓ CORRECT

Explanation: Deep hypotaxis can suggest complexity, qualification, or entanglement—context determines which reading is most appropriate. No single interpretation is inherently correct.

Question 5251 [English] - ID: exp021
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Figurative Language
------------------------------------------------------------
The author employs catachresis (a deliberately 'wrong' metaphor) in 'the voice of the wind' to:

  A. personify nature through impossible attribution ✓ CORRECT
  B. extend language beyond literal capacity
  C. create fresh perception through category violation
  D. blur the boundary between animate and inanimate

Explanation: Catachresis deliberately violates categorical boundaries to personify nature—attributing human voice to wind where no proper term exists.

Question 5252 [English] - ID: exp022
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Rhetorical Devices
------------------------------------------------------------
Which analysis best captures the function of the passage's hysteron proteron ('I die, I faint, I fail')?

  A. temporal reversal for dramatic climax positioning
  B. anti-climactic ordering suggesting consciousness dissolution ✓ CORRECT
  C. logical reversal mimicking perceptual priority under stress
  D. inverted sequence reflecting disoriented mental state

Explanation: Hysteron proteron (putting the logically later thing first) creates anti-climax here—the sequence moves from death to lesser states, suggesting consciousness fading.

Question 5253 [English] - ID: exp023

------------------------------------------------------------
The passage's use of aporia ('I know not whether to call it courage or foolishness') functions to:

  A. express genuine uncertainty about classification
  B. invite reader participation in judgment
  C. suggest the inadequacy of binary categories ✓ CORRECT
  D. demonstrate intellectual humility

Explanation: Aporia (expression of doubt) here suggests the inadequacy of binary categories—the speaker indicates that neither term fully captures the complexity.

Question 5254 [English] - ID: exp024

------------------------------------------------------------
The author's epanorthosis ('He was brave—no, reckless—in his defiance') achieves:

  A. self-correction that intensifies rather than retracts ✓ CORRECT
  B. performed deliberation that carries both terms forward
  C. rhetorical refinement that sharpens judgment
  D. progressive specification from general to precise

Explanation: Epanorthosis (self-correction) intensifies rather than retracts—'reckless' is stronger than 'brave,' so the correction escalates the characterization.

Question 5259 [English] - ID: exp029
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Rhetorical Devices
------------------------------------------------------------
The author's antimetabole ('When the going gets tough, the tough get going') differs from chiasmus in that:

  A. it repeats words rather than just reversing structure ✓ CORRECT
  B. the repetition involves semantic shift between uses
  C. the reversal creates logical rather than merely formal symmetry
  D. it produces a proverbial or aphoristic quality

Explanation: Antimetabole differs from chiasmus by repeating actual words—'tough' and 'going' appear twice, whereas chiasmus merely reverses grammatical structure.

Question 5260 [English] - ID: exp030

The passage's brachylogy ('The more, the merrier') achieves its effect through:

  A. elliptical compression requiring inferential completion
  B. proverbial form suggesting universal wisdom
  C. comparative structure implying proportional relationship ✓ CORRECT
  D. memorable brevity enhancing quotability

Explanation: Brachylogy (compressed expression) implies proportional relationship—'the more [people], the merrier [the occasion]' suggests direct correlation between variables.

  A. recursive self-reference problematizing representation
  B. infinite regress suggesting the impossibility of closure
  C. meta-textual commentary on the nature of storytelling
  D. structural mirroring between embedded and framing narratives ✓ CORRECT

Explanation: Mise en abyme (story within story mirroring the whole) creates structural mirroring—the embedded narrative reflects and comments on the framing narrative.

The author's paralepsis ('I will pass over in silence his criminal record') combines with apophasis to create:

  A. double negation that emphatically affirms
  B. rhetorical plausible deniability
  C. ironic performance of restraint that exercises none ✓ CORRECT
  D. accusation disguised as diplomatic omission

Explanation: Paralepsis and apophasis together perform restraint ironically—the speaker claims silence while actually speaking, exercising no restraint at all.

  A. destabilize the ontological boundaries of fiction ✓ CORRECT
  B. foreground the constructedness of narrative
  C. invite reflection on reader-text-author relationships
  D. create postmodern self-awareness in the text

  C. readerly disorientation as deliberate aesthetic effect
  D. chronological disruption emphasizing memory's non-linearity

  A. effaces agency to create atmosphere of inevitability ✓ CORRECT
  B. shifts focus from actor to affected entity
  C. creates syntactic ambiguity between active and middle voice
  D. suggests events occurring without human causation

  C. refusal of causal logic that would impose meaning on meaninglessness ✓ CORRECT
  D. formal resistance to narrative coherence

  D. it demands sustained interpretive vigilance from readers ✓ CORRECT

------------------------------------------------------------

  A. technical

  C. neutral

Explanation: 'Rhetorical' refers to language used for persuasive effect, particularly appealing to emotions.

  B. lacks coherent principles or beliefs
  C. deliberately cultivates an air of mystery
  D. struggles with indecision on important matters

Question 5343 [English] - ID: eng_hard2_003

  B. It creates a more formal academic tone
  C. It obscures the identity of the antagonist
  D. It maintains objectivity in the narrative

Explanation: Passive voice constructions remove the subject as the active agent, which when used deliberately emphasizes how events happen to someone rather than being controlled by them.

Question 5350 [English] - ID: eng_hard2_010

------------------------------------------------------------
The author's use of litotes in describing the discovery as 'not insignificant' serves to

  A. understate importance while actually emphasizing it ✓ CORRECT
  B. express genuine uncertainty about the discovery's value
  C. criticize others who have overstated its importance
  D. maintain scientific objectivity in the assessment

Explanation: Litotes (understatement through negation) is a rhetorical device that often emphasizes by appearing to diminish, making 'not insignificant' stronger than simply 'significant.'

  B. confusion about who is being discussed
  C. suspense about the subject's identity
  D. distance between the reader and the subject

Question 5352 [English] - ID: eng_hard2_012

------------------------------------------------------------
The recursive structure of the sentence 'She remembered remembering' most effectively conveys

  A. the layered nature of memory and consciousness ✓ CORRECT
  B. the character's excellent recall abilities
  C. a grammatical error in the original text
  D. the character's obsessive tendencies

Explanation: The nested structure mirrors the psychological phenomenon of meta-memory—awareness of one's own memory processes—suggesting depth and complexity in consciousness.

Question 5353 [English] - ID: eng_hard2_013
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Figurative Language
------------------------------------------------------------
The author employs synecdoche when referring to 'the crown' to

  A. represent the monarchy as an institution through a symbolic object ✓ CORRECT
  B. describe the physical appearance of the ruler
  C. emphasize the material wealth of the kingdom
  D. create a visual image for readers to remember

Explanation: Synecdoche uses a part to represent the whole; 'the crown' stands for the entire monarchical system, not just the physical object.


================================================================================

Question 5354 [English] - ID: eng_hard2_014
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Rhetorical Devices
------------------------------------------------------------
The author's strategic repetition of 'and yet' at the beginning of three consecutive paragraphs serves to

  A. accumulate contradictions that complicate a simple interpretation ✓ CORRECT
  B. signal a lack of original transitional phrases
  C. create a poetic rhythm in expository prose
  D. indicate the author's ambivalence about the topic

Explanation: Anaphora (repetition at paragraph beginnings) with a contrastive phrase builds a pattern of complicating factors, suggesting the subject resists easy categorization.

Question 5355 [English] - ID: eng_hard2_015
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Rhetorical Devices
------------------------------------------------------------
The chiasmus in 'we shape our tools and thereafter our tools shape us' emphasizes

  A. the reciprocal relationship between humans and technology ✓ CORRECT
  B. the superiority of human creativity over mechanical function
  C. the inevitable dominance of technology
  D. the author's skill with rhetorical figures

Explanation: Chiasmus (ABBA pattern) structurally mirrors the mutual influence described, with the reversal of subject and object reinforcing the concept of reciprocity.

  A. provide citations for claims made in the main text
  B. offer personal commentary that qualifies or complicates the main argument ✓ CORRECT
  C. define technical terms for general readers
  D. summarize points made in previous paragraphs

Explanation: Parenthetical insertions often represent the author's secondary voice, allowing for personal reflection or qualification without disrupting the main argument's flow.

Question 5364 [English] - ID: eng_hard2_024

------------------------------------------------------------
The zeugma in 'she lowered her expectations and her voice' achieves its effect by

  A. creating grammatical confusion
  B. linking abstract and concrete concepts through shared syntax ✓ CORRECT
  C. emphasizing the character's contradictory nature
  D. demonstrating the flexibility of English grammar

Explanation: Zeugma uses one verb to govern two objects of different types (here, abstract 'expectations' and physical 'voice'), creating surprise through unexpected connection.

  A. a grammatical error in the original text
  B. a move from describing reality to contemplating possibility ✓ CORRECT
  C. increasing formality in the author's tone
  D. the author's uncertainty about basic grammar

Explanation: The subjunctive mood expresses hypotheticals, wishes, or possibilities; shifting to it signals a move from describing what is to imagining what might be.

  A. genuine restraint in avoiding certain topics
  B. confusion about what is being discussed
  C. emphasis on the very thing supposedly being set aside ✓ CORRECT
  D. respectful treatment of sensitive subjects

  B. transition smoothly between paragraphs

  B. limiting the narrative to physical action
  C. linking diverse scenes through thematic resonance ✓ CORRECT

  A. redundancy through repetition
  B. confusion about the actual meaning
  C. rhetorical power through rhythmic emphasis and completeness ✓ CORRECT

Question 5385 [English] - ID: eng_hard2_045

------------------------------------------------------------
The diegetic narrator, who exists within the story world, differs from an extradiegetic narrator primarily in

  A. reliability of information
  B. use of first-person pronouns
  C. position relative to the events being narrated ✓ CORRECT
  D. length of narrative passages

Explanation: Diegetic narrators exist within the story world as characters; extradiegetic narrators exist outside it. The key difference is their position relative to events.

  A. confuse the central argument
  B. demonstrate vocabulary range
  C. appeal to readers' emotions
  D. sharpen conceptual distinctions through direct contrast ✓ CORRECT

  A. demonstrate supernatural elements
  B. confuse the reader about who is speaking
  C. avoid stating the author's own opinion
  D. dramatize ideas by embodying them in speech ✓ CORRECT

  A. the author's inconsistent voice

Question 5397 [English] - ID: eng_hard2_057

The focalizer in this passage, through whose perspective events are filtered, is distinct from the narrator because

  A. the focalizer speaks directly to readers
  B. there can only be one perspective in any narrative
  C. the narrator controls the focalizer's thoughts
  D. the narrator tells while the focalizer perceives ✓ CORRECT

  B. confusion about priorities
  C. respect for the reader's time
  D. emphasis through apparent dismissal ✓ CORRECT

Explanation: Paralepsis draws attention to something by pretending to ignore it, achieving emphasis through the pretense of omission.

  A. poor grasp of grammar
  B. careless revision

  D. the non-linear nature of spontaneous thought ✓ CORRECT

Question 5400 [English] - ID: eng_hard2_060
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Sentence Structure
------------------------------------------------------------
The paratactic structure of the passage, using coordination rather than subordination, creates

  A. complexity through embedded clauses
  B. a hierarchical arrangement of ideas
  C. difficulty in following the argument
  D. a sense of equivalence among the connected elements ✓ CORRECT

Explanation: Parataxis (coordination without subordination) presents ideas as equal, without ranking them hierarchically, creating a flatter, more democratic structure.


================================================================================

Question 5402 [English] - ID: sat-p1-002
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Command of Evidence
------------------------------------------------------------
Studying tools unearthed at a cave site on the western coast of Italy, archaeologist Paola Villa and colleagues have determined that prehistoric Neanderthal groups fashioned them from shells of clams that they harvested from the seafloor while wading or diving or that washed up on the beach. Clamshells become thin and eroded as they wash up on the beach, while those on the seafloor are smooth and sturdy, so the research team suspects that Neanderthals prized the tools made with seafloor shells. However, the team also concluded that those tools were likely more challenging to obtain. Which choice most effectively uses data to support the research team's conclusion?

  A. At each depth below the surface in the cave, the difference in the numbers of tools of each type suggests that shells were easier to collect from the beach than to harvest from the seafloor. ✓ CORRECT
  B. The highest number of tools were at a depth of 3–4 meters below the surface, which suggests that the Neanderthal population at the site was highest during the related period of time.
  C. At each depth below the surface in the cave, the difference in the numbers of tools of each type suggests that Neanderthals preferred to use clamshells from the beach because of their durability.
  D. The higher number of tools at depths of 5–6 meters below the surface in the cave than at depths of 4–5 meters below the surface suggests that the size of clam populations changed over time.

Explanation: The fact that beach shells (less desirable) outnumber seafloor shells (more desirable) in the archaeological record suggests that seafloor shells were harder to obtain, supporting the researchers' conclusion.


================================================================================

Question 5403 [English] - ID: sat-p1-003
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Inferences
------------------------------------------------------------
Many of William Shakespeare's tragedies address broad themes that still appeal to today's audiences. For instance, Romeo and Juliet, which is set in the Italy of Shakespeare's time, tackles the themes of parents versus children and love versus hate, and the play continues to be read and produced widely around the world. But understanding Shakespeare's so-called history plays can require a knowledge of several centuries of English history. Consequently, ______. Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. many theatergoers and readers today are likely to find Shakespeare's history plays less engaging than the tragedies. ✓ CORRECT
  B. some of Shakespeare's tragedies are more relevant to today's audiences than twentieth-century plays.
  C. Romeo and Juliet is the most thematically accessible of all Shakespeare's tragedies.
  D. experts in English history tend to prefer Shakespeare's history plays to his other works.

Explanation: The text contrasts tragedies (universal themes, accessible) with history plays (require historical knowledge). Since most readers lack extensive knowledge of English history, they would find history plays less engaging.


================================================================================

Question 5404 [English] - ID: sat-p1-004
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Command of Evidence
------------------------------------------------------------
Three studies estimated the average velocity of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC): Murai and Fujimoto (1980) at 344 km/s, Gardiner and colleagues (1994) at 297 km/s, and Kallivayalil and colleagues (2006) at 378 km/s. Before the 2006 study, estimates were low enough for the LMC to maintain an orbit around the Milky Way, but according to Gurtina Besla and colleagues, the 2006 estimate is too high for the LMC to maintain such an orbit. Therefore, if Besla and colleagues are correct, the maximum average velocity for the LMC that would allow it to maintain orbit around the Milky Way is likely ______. Which choice most effectively completes the statement?

  A. above 344 km/s but below 378 km/s. ✓ CORRECT
  B. above 297 km/s but below 344 km/s.
  C. above 378 km/s.
  D. below 297 km/s.

Explanation: The 1980 estimate (344 km/s) was within orbital range, but the 2006 estimate (378 km/s) exceeded it. Therefore, the maximum orbital velocity must be between these two values.


================================================================================

Question 5406 [English] - ID: sat-p1-006
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Inferences
------------------------------------------------------------
Anthropologist Judith Becker studied Pentecostal church services and Sufi dhikr ceremonies, both of which use repeated rhythms, music, and movement. She argues that these practices induce altered states of consciousness by disrupting normal patterns of sensory processing. A neuroscientist critiquing this view might point out that ______. Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. similar neurological changes can occur in contexts that lack the specific rhythmic and musical elements Becker identifies as crucial. ✓ CORRECT
  B. the specific patterns of brain activity associated with religious experiences have been well documented across multiple cultures.
  C. participants in these ceremonies often report that the experience feels profoundly meaningful to them.
  D. repeated rhythms and music are found in religious practices across many different cultures and historical periods.

Explanation: A critique would challenge whether the specific mechanisms Becker identifies are necessary. (A) does this by suggesting altered states can occur without those specific elements.

Question 5416 [English] - ID: sat-p1-016
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Command of Evidence
------------------------------------------------------------
Jean-Bernard Caron and colleagues recently discovered a cache of jellyfish fossils in the Burgess Shale, a site in the Canadian Rockies that is rich in fossils from the Cambrian period. Caron and colleagues claim that these are the oldest jellyfish fossils ever discovered. In the past twenty years, two sites in China and the United States have yielded fossils of a similar age that some experts believe are most likely jellyfish due to their shapes and the appearance of projecting tentacles. But Caron and colleagues argue that the apparent tentacles are in fact the comb rows of ctenophores, gelatinous animals that are only distantly related to jellyfish. Which statement, if true, would most directly weaken the claim by Caron and colleagues about the fossils found in China and the United States?

  A. Sites in the Canadian Rockies from later periods than the Cambrian period have yielded fossils that have been conclusively identified as ctenophore fossils.
  B. The fossils found in China and the United States are so poorly preserved that though they cannot be conclusively identified as jellyfish, they cannot be conclusively identified as ctenophores either. ✓ CORRECT
  C. While ctenophore fossils have been discovered in China and the United States, they have never been discovered in the Burgess Shale.
  D. The fossils discovered by Caron and colleagues in the Burgess Shale were better preserved than the fossils discovered by other researchers in China and the United States.

Explanation: If the fossils cannot be conclusively identified as ctenophores, Caron's claim that they are ctenophores (not jellyfish) would be weakened since the identification is uncertain.

Question 5417 [English] - ID: sat-p1-017
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Command of Evidence
------------------------------------------------------------
Almost all works of fiction contain references to the progression of time, including the time of day when events take place. In a 2020 study, Allen Kim, Charuta Pethe, and Steven Skiena claim that an observable pattern in such references reflects a shift in human behavior prompted by the spread of electric lighting in the late nineteenth century. The researchers analyzed more than 50,000 novels spanning many centuries and cultures. Which finding from the study, if true, would most directly support the researchers' conclusion?

  A. Novels published after the year 1800 include the clock phrase 10 a.m. less often than novels published before the year 1800 do.
  B. Novels published after 1880 contain significantly more references to activities occurring after 10 p.m. than do novels from earlier periods. ✓ CORRECT
  C. Among novels published in the nineteenth century, implied time references become steadily more common than clock phrases as publication dates approach 1900.
  D. The time references of noon (12 p.m.) and midnight (12 a.m.) are used with roughly the same frequency in the novels.

Explanation: Electric lighting enabled activities after dark. If novels after 1880 (when electric lighting spread) show more late-night activity references, this supports the claim that lighting changed behavior.

  B. The proportion of municipalities that responded to the inquiry or offered incentives didn't substantially differ across the announcement timing conditions. ✓ CORRECT

  D. Of the municipalities that received an inquiry mentioning plans for an announcement date after the next election, more than 1,200 didn't respond and only around 100 offered incentives.

Question 5431 [English] - ID: sat-p1-031
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Inferences
------------------------------------------------------------
To understand how temperature change affects microorganism-mediated cycling of soil nutrients in alpine ecosystems, Eva Kaštovská et al. collected plant-soil cores in the Tatra Mountains at elevations around 2,100 meters and transplanted them to elevations of 1,700–1,800 meters, where the mean air temperature was warmer by 2°C. Microorganism-mediated nutrient cycling was accelerated in the transplanted cores; crucially, microorganism community composition was unchanged, allowing Kaštovská et al. to attribute the acceleration to temperature-induced increases in microorganism activity. The finding about the microorganism community composition was important for which reason?

  A. It provided preliminary evidence that microorganism-mediated nutrient cycling was accelerated in the transplanted cores.
  B. It suggested that temperature-induced changes in microorganism activity may be occurring at increasingly high elevations.
  C. It ruled out a potential alternative explanation for the acceleration in microorganism-mediated nutrient cycling. ✓ CORRECT
  D. It clarified that microorganism activity levels in the plant-soil cores varied depending on which microorganisms comprised the community.

Explanation: If the community composition had changed, accelerated nutrient cycling could be due to different microorganisms rather than temperature effects. Unchanged composition rules out this alternative.

Audience members, who wander through the labyrinthine venue at their own pace and follow the actors as they play out simultaneous, interweaving narrative loops, confront the impossibility of experiencing the production in its entirety. The play's refusal of narrative coherence thus hinges on the sense of spatial fragmentation that the venue's immense and intricate layout generates. What does the text most strongly suggest about Sleep No More's use of its performance space?

  A. The choice of a New York City venue likely enabled the play's creators to experiment with the use of theatrical space in a way that venues from earlier productions could not.
  B. Audience members likely find the experience of the play disappointing because they generally cannot make their way through the entire venue.
  C. The production's dependence on a particular performance environment would likely make it difficult to reproduce exactly in a different theatrical space. ✓ CORRECT
  D. Audience members who navigate the space according to a recommended itinerary will likely have a better grasp of the play's narrative than audience members who depart from that itinerary.

Question 5433 [English] - ID: sat-p1-033
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Command of Evidence
------------------------------------------------------------
Mosasaurs were large marine reptiles that lived in the Late Cretaceous period. Celina Suarez, Alberto Pérez-Huerta, and T. Lynn Harrell Jr. examined oxygen-18 isotopes in mosasaur tooth enamel to calculate likely body temperatures and determined that mosasaurs were endothermic—they used internal metabolic processes to maintain a stable body temperature. The researchers claim that endothermy would have enabled mosasaurs to include relatively cold polar waters in their range. Which finding, if true, would most directly support this claim?

  A. Mosasaurs' likely body temperatures are easier to determine from tooth enamel oxygen-18 isotope data than the body temperatures of nonendothermic Late Cretaceous marine reptiles are.
  B. Fossils of both mosasaurs and nonendothermic marine reptiles have been found in roughly equal numbers in regions known to be near the poles during the Late Cretaceous.
  C. Several mosasaur fossils have been found in regions known to be near the poles during the Late Cretaceous, while relatively few fossils of nonendothermic marine reptiles have been found in those locations. ✓ CORRECT
  D. During the Late Cretaceous, seawater temperatures were likely higher throughout mosasaurs' range, including near the poles, than seawater temperatures at those same latitudes are today.

Explanation: If endothermy enabled mosasaurs to live in cold polar waters, we would expect mosasaur fossils in polar regions but fewer fossils of nonendothermic reptiles there. (C) describes exactly this pattern.

Question 5434 [English] - ID: sat-p1-034
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Command of Evidence
------------------------------------------------------------
Research suggests that REM sleep in animals is homeostatically regulated: animals compensate for periods of REM sleep deprivation by increasing subsequent REM sleep. When on land, fur seals get enough REM sleep, but during the weeks they're in the water, they get almost none. Researchers studied fur seals' sleep habits after they returned to land and concluded that REM sleep may not be homeostatically regulated in fur seals. Which choice most effectively uses data to support this conclusion?

  A. The seals didn't show significantly less REM sleep during the second day after returning to land than they did during the first day.
  B. The seals showed no significant differences from one another in baseline levels of REM sleep.
  C. The seals didn't consistently demonstrate a significant increase in REM sleep after their period of deprivation in the water. ✓ CORRECT
  D. There was no significant difference between REM sleep after returning to land and REM sleep while in the water.

Explanation: If REM sleep were homeostatically regulated, seals would compensate for weeks of deprivation with significantly increased REM. If they didn't consistently show this increase, homeostatic regulation is unlikely.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. Many visual phenomena can be explained entirely in terms of neural activity without reference to bodily movement.
  B. The brain processes visual information in distinct regions that have been mapped through neuroimaging.
  C. Patients with paralysis who cannot move their bodies still report having visual experiences. ✓ CORRECT
  D. Different species have evolved different visual systems adapted to their particular environments.

Question 5436 [English] - ID: sat-p1-036
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Craft and Structure | Skill: Text Structure and Purpose
------------------------------------------------------------
Geologist Marie Tharp's detailed mapping of the ocean floor in the 1950s revealed the Mid-Atlantic Ridge and the rift valley running along it. Her work provided crucial evidence for the theory of plate tectonics, yet she faced significant resistance from the male-dominated scientific establishment of her era. Historian of science Naomi Oreskes notes that Tharp's experience illustrates a broader pattern in which ______. Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. Geological discoveries have frequently been made by scientists working outside major research institutions.
  B. The development of new technologies often precedes major advances in scientific theory.
  C. The reception of scientific work has sometimes been influenced by the social position of the scientist rather than just the quality of the evidence. ✓ CORRECT
  D. Oceanographic research has historically received less funding than other scientific fields.

Explanation: Tharp's work provided strong evidence but faced resistance due to her gender. This exemplifies how scientists' social position can affect how their work is received.

------------------------------------------------------------
Linguist George Lakoff has argued that human thought is fundamentally metaphorical—we understand abstract concepts through their relationships to concrete, embodied experiences. For example, we conceptualize time in terms of spatial movement ("the future is ahead of us"). This framework suggests that ______. Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. Languages that use different spatial metaphors for time may produce different intuitions about temporal relationships. ✓ CORRECT
  B. Abstract reasoning developed later in human evolution than spatial cognition.
  C. The relationship between language and thought is one of the oldest questions in philosophy.
  D. Children learn concrete vocabulary before they learn words for abstract concepts.

Explanation: If abstract concepts are understood through spatial metaphors, and different languages use different metaphors, speakers might have different intuitions. (A) follows from Lakoff's framework.

A critic might respond that ______. Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. Many women artists did receive training and produce significant work that was simply overlooked by male art historians.
  B. The definition of "greatness" in art has itself been shaped by values and perspectives that excluded women.
  C. Both (A) and (B) address limitations in Nochlin's argument that could deepen the analysis. ✓ CORRECT
  D. Artistic genius is an innate quality that institutional training cannot produce or inhibit.

Some economists have challenged his methodology, but the broader implication of his analysis is that ______. Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. Progressive taxation is the only effective tool for reducing wealth inequality.
  B. Wealth concentration was more extreme in the nineteenth century than it is today.
  C. Left unchecked, market economies may tend toward increasing rather than decreasing inequality. ✓ CORRECT
  D. Most economists agree that some level of inequality is necessary for economic growth.

  B. The accumulation of genetic and structural evidence can ultimately overcome initial resistance to unconventional ideas. ✓ CORRECT

Question 5443 [English] - ID: sat-p1-043
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Command of Evidence
------------------------------------------------------------
Susanne A. Benz and colleagues analyzed thousands of sites on three continents, examining whether subsurface thermal pollution (excess heat) could contribute to home heating needs. Under the current surface temperature scenario, more than 80% of sites cannot use thermal pollution for any portion of local home heating needs, but at the maximum plausible surface temperature, that percentage drops below 20%. Which choice best describes data that support Benz and colleagues' conclusion that thermal pollution could increasingly contribute to meeting home heating needs?

  A. Under both temperature conditions, less than 10% of sites were in the up-to-25% group, but at the maximum plausible surface temperature, almost 80% of sites could have all their local heating needs met by thermal pollution.
  B. At current surface temperatures, more than 80% of the sites have no need for supplemental local home heating from subsurface thermal pollution.
  C. At current surface temperatures, more than 80% of sites cannot use thermal pollution to meet any portion of local home heating needs, but at maximum plausible surface temperature, that percentage drops below 20%. ✓ CORRECT
  D. At current surface temperatures, more than 80% of sites can meet, at most, 25% of local home heating needs with subsurface thermal pollution.

Explanation: The conclusion is that as temperatures approach maximum plausible levels, more sites can use thermal pollution. (C) directly supports this by showing the percentage of sites that can't use it drops from 80%+ to under 20%.

------------------------------------------------------------
The following text is from a 1913 poem. A zoo is described: "There, the lion, not to be restrained, still paces in his cage, / A symbol of the wild, majestic rage / He might have shown if free in his domain— / But now he paces endless lengths in vain. / That lion with his lordly, untamed heart / Has in some man his human counterpart, / Some lofty soul in dreams and visions wrapped, / But in the stifling flesh securely trapped." Based on the text, what challenge do humans sometimes experience?

  A. They cannot effectively tame certain wild animals because of a lack of compassion.
  B. They cannot focus on setting attainable goals because of a lack of motivation.
  C. They quickly become frustrated when faced with difficult tasks because of a lack of self-control.
  D. They have aspirations that cannot be fulfilled because of certain limitations. ✓ CORRECT

Explanation: The poem draws a parallel between a caged lion and a "lofty soul...trapped." The lion has untamed potential but is confined; likewise, humans have dreams but are "trapped" by limitations.

This analysis implies that ______. Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. Schools in lower-income communities receive less funding than schools in wealthy areas.
  B. Students from disadvantaged backgrounds are inherently less capable than their more privileged peers.
  C. Educational success depends partly on factors that precede and extend beyond formal schooling.
  D. Truly meritocratic education would require addressing cultural factors that schools currently take for granted. ✓ CORRECT

Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. Antidiscrimination law should address only the most severe forms of discrimination.
  B. Women of color face challenges that are distinct from those faced by white women or men of color separately.
  C. Most individuals experience only one form of discrimination at a time.
  D. Policies designed to address only one form of discrimination may be insufficient for those at the intersection of multiple categories. ✓ CORRECT

Explanation: Crenshaw's point is that single-axis analysis (just race OR just gender) misses the experience of those facing multiple disadvantages. Policies addressing only one dimension may therefore be inadequate.

Between revolutions, scientists work within established paradigms, often resisting anomalies that don't fit. Kuhn's analysis suggests that ______. Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. Scientific education should expose students to multiple competing paradigms simultaneously.
  B. The history of science shows a pattern of continuous, incremental progress toward truth.
  C. Most scientific work involves solving problems within accepted frameworks rather than questioning fundamental assumptions.
  D. Scientists who challenge prevailing assumptions may face resistance from the scientific community before their ideas are accepted. ✓ CORRECT

Explanation: Kuhn emphasized that scientists within paradigms resist anomalies and challenges. This means scientists proposing paradigm shifts face resistance until sufficient crisis forces reconsideration.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. Anthropologists can never fully understand cultures different from their own.
  B. Quantitative data is more reliable than qualitative observation.
  C. The same observable action can have different meanings in different cultural contexts. ✓ CORRECT
  D. Human behavior is governed by universal laws that apply across all cultures.

  A. Authors should avoid using dialogue in their fiction.
  B. Literary texts contain multiple voices and perspectives in tension with each other. ✓ CORRECT
  C. Readers' interpretations of a text are irrelevant to its meaning.
  D. Novels with first-person narration are more authentic than those with third-person narration.

Explanation: Dialogism emphasizes that meaning emerges through interaction of multiple voices. Applied to literature, this suggests texts contain multiple perspectives in dialogue, not a single authoritative voice.

Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. Organizations with captive constituencies may be more responsive to member concerns than those whose members can easily leave.
  B. Employee turnover rates are higher in industries with many competing firms.
  C. Political systems that restrict emigration tend to experience less internal dissent.
  D. Some organizations may actually deteriorate as their most committed members leave rather than fight for reform. ✓ CORRECT

  A. Scientific objectivity is impossible to achieve because all knowledge is shaped by social position.
  B. Marginalized groups have unique access to empirical data unavailable to researchers from dominant groups.
  C. The experiences of dominant groups are irrelevant to understanding social reality.
  D. Starting from marginalized standpoints can generate questions and insights that might not emerge from dominant perspectives. ✓ CORRECT

Question 5461 [English] - ID: satp2001
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Inferences
------------------------------------------------------------
One theory behind human bipedalism speculates that it originated in a mostly ground-based ancestor that practiced four-legged 'knuckle-walking,' like chimpanzees and gorillas do today, and eventually evolved into moving upright on two legs. But recently, researchers observed orangutans, another relative of humans, standing on two legs on tree branches and using their arms for balance while they reached for fruits. These observations may suggest that ______

Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. bipedalism evolved because it was advantageous to a tree-dwelling ancestor of humans. ✓ CORRECT
  B. bipedalism must have evolved simultaneously with knuckle-walking and tree-climbing.
  C. moving between the ground and the trees would have been difficult without bipedalism.
  D. a knuckle-walking human ancestor could have easily moved bipedally in trees.

Question 5462 [English] - ID: satp2002
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Command of Evidence
------------------------------------------------------------
Neural networks are computer models intended to reflect the organization of human brains and are often used in studies of brain function. According to an analysis of 11,000 such networks, Rylan Schaeffer and colleagues advise caution when drawing conclusions about brains from observations of neural networks. They found that when attempting to mimic grid cells, while 90% of the networks could accomplish navigation-related tasks, only about 10% of those exhibited any behaviors similar to those of grid cells. But even this approximation of grid-cell activity has less to do with similarity between the neural networks and biological brains than it does with the rules programmed into the networks.

Which finding, if true, would most directly support the underlined claim?

  A. The rules that allow for networks to exhibit behaviors like those of grid cells have no equivalent in the function of biological brains. ✓ CORRECT
  B. The networks that do not exhibit behaviors like those of grid cells were nonetheless programmed with rules that had proven useful in earlier neural-network studies.
  C. Neural networks can often accomplish tasks that biological brains do, but they are typically programmed with rules to model multiple types of brain cells simultaneously.
  D. Once a neural network is programmed, it is trained on certain tasks to see if it can independently arrive at processes that are similar to those performed by biological brains.

Explanation: While many networks can perform navigation tasks, or even mimic grid cells, it doesn't mean they're actually behaving like biological brains—this finding suggests that the rules that govern neural network behavior are completely unlike the way real brains work.

Question 5464 [English] - ID: satp2004
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Inferences
------------------------------------------------------------
The Hubble Space Telescope (HST) is projected to maintain operation until at least 2030, but it has already revolutionized high-resolution imaging of solar-system bodies in visible and ultraviolet (UV) light wavelengths, notwithstanding that only about 6% of the bodies imaged by the HST are within the solar system. NASA researcher Cindy L. Young and colleagues assert that a new space telescope dedicated exclusively to solar-system observations would permit an extensive survey of minor solar-system bodies and long-term UV observation to discern how solar-system bodies change over time. Young and colleagues' recommendation therefore implies that the HST ______

Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. will likely continue to be used primarily to observe objects outside the solar system. ✓ CORRECT
  B. will no longer be used to observe solar system objects if the telescope recommended by Young and colleagues is deployed.
  C. can be modified to observe the features of solar system objects that are of interest to Young and colleagues.
  D. lacks the sensors to observe the wavelengths of light needed to discern how solar system bodies change over time.

Explanation: The HST will operate until at least 2030, but it's only observing stuff inside our solar system 6% of the time. If we could get a different telescope to observe stuff inside our solar system 100% of the time and take more extensive images of certain things, then the HST could continue to be used mainly for observing stuff outside the solar system.

Question 5465 [English] - ID: satp2005
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Inferences
------------------------------------------------------------
Geoglyphs are large-scale designs of lines or shapes created in a natural landscape. The Nazca Lines were created in the Nazca Desert in Peru by several Indigenous civilizations over a period of many centuries. Peruvian archaeologist Johny Isla specializes in these geoglyphs. At a German exhibit about the Nazca Lines, he saw an old photograph of a large geoglyph of a whalelike figure and was surprised that he didn't recognize it. Isla returned to Peru and used a drone to search a wide area, looking for the figure from the air. This approach suggests that Isla thought that if he hadn't already seen it, the whalelike geoglyph ______

Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. must represent a species of whale that went extinct before there were any people in Peru.
  B. is actually located in Germany, not Peru, and isn't part of the Nazca Lines at all.
  C. is probably in a location Isla hadn't ever come across while on the ground. ✓ CORRECT
  D. was almost certainly created a long time after the other Nazca Lines geoglyphs were created.

Explanation: Given his expertise, and his surprise at being unfamiliar with the whale glyph, the text strongly suggests that Isla believed he would have noticed the glyph if he had been to its location. Thus, the text implies that the whalelike geoglyph is likely in a location Isla had not previously been to.

Question 5466 [English] - ID: satp2006
Difficulty: Hard | Domain: Information and Ideas | Skill: Inferences
------------------------------------------------------------
In 2022, Elizabeth Reeck and colleagues studied whether the decision-making modes that guide consumers influence their choice between nonenvironmentally friendly standard electricity plans and environmentally friendly green plans that cap electricity usage. Study participants who self-reported using either an Affect Mode or Role Mode—which prioritize choices that have a stronger positive emotional or social impact, respectively—were more likely to select a green plan. Conversely, participants using a Calculation Mode—which aims to minimize both financial cost and personal inconvenience—were more likely to select a standard plan, even when the green option was cheaper. This finding suggests that participants using a Calculation Mode ______

Which choice most logically completes the text?

  A. were equally unlikely to factor the financial savings of the green plan into their decision-making as were participants using either the Affect or Role Modes.
  B. may have been less strongly motivated to appear socially responsible with their choice of plan than they realized.
  C. may have determined that the green plan imposed additional burdens on them that were not sufficiently offset by the potential financial savings. ✓ CORRECT
  D. were less likely to believe that the green plan was truly cost-effective than were participants using either the Affect or Role Modes.

Explanation: If those using a Calculation Mode disfavor the green plan, it would be due to some financial or convenience burden the green plan imposes that the other doesn't. The text indicates that the green plan had a lower financial cost but was nonetheless rejected by participants using the Calculation Mode. It therefore follows that the green plan likely imposed a convenience burden that outweighed potential financial savings for these participants.
